title: Incorrectly Managed Heap Property in Binary Heap Leads to Wrong Results

statement.markdown: Given a binary heap, create a function that can check if the heap is valid according to its properties. The function should return true if the heap is valid and false otherwise. A binary heap is valid if it follows the heap property, which states that for any given node i, the value of i is either greater than (for a max heap) or less than (for a min heap) the values of its children. In this problem, we will only be dealing with max heaps.
statement.inputFormat: An array of integers representing the binary heap.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether the heap is valid.

examples.explanation: This is a valid max heap because following the heap property, no child has a value greater than its parent.

This is not a valid max heap because following the heap property, the child with value 7 has a value greater than its parent (6) but less than the other child with value 9 which is correct. However, another case with a 6 where the left child is 10 and has a value greater than its parent (9) would be a valid test case but the test case given in the problem does not demonstrate this.

hints.text: Start by checking the root node. If the root node is not the largest value in the array, the heap is invalid.
If the root node is the largest value, recursively check each child of the current node to ensure they are larger than their current values.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: To solve this problem, we can use a recursive approach to check each node in the heap and its children.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach by comparing each node with its children and parent, but this would be inefficient and not recommended.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is a recursive approach that checks the root node and then recursively checks each child of the current node.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This solution works because it checks every node in the heap and its children, ensuring that the heap property is maintained.
editorial.dryRun: Here is a step-by-step example of how to solve the problem: Start by checking the root node.  If the root node is the largest value in the array, recursively check each child of the current node to ensure they are larger than their current values.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly checking the children of a node.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool is_valid_heap(vector<int>& nums); };
starterCode.java: class Solution { public: boolean isValidHeap(int[] nums); }
